results.title: Surge AI is latest San Francisco startup accused of ...
results.content: The Scale AI logo is seen displayed on a smartphone screen next to a laptop keyboard. Artificial intelligence training company Surge AI was hit with a lawsuit Tuesday alleging it misclassified its workers, denying them benefits given to employees. Artificial intelligence training company Surge AI has been hit with a lawsuit alleging it has misclassified contractors hired to improve chat responses from AI software for some of the world’s leading tech companies. The proposed class action lawsuit alleges that “data annotators” — hired by Surge AI to ensure that powerful AI systems run by Meta and OpenAI can properly generate text responses that are accurate and capable of mimicking human expressions — were “deliberately” classified as independent contractors, denying them benefits given to employees. Similar lawsuits have been filed against Scale AI, a larger AI training company that has gathered a vast workforce of contractors to train AI tools for companies, including Open AI and Google, as well as the U.S. Department of Defense.

results.title: AI Training Firm Surge AI Hit With Worker Misclassification Suit
results.content: ### Bloomberg. Connecting decision makers to a dynamic network of information, people and ideas, Bloomberg quickly and accurately delivers business and financial information, news and insight around the world. ### Products. ### Media Services. The artificial intelligence training platform Surge Labs Inc., which hires “data annotators” to help improve AI model responses, was sued in California state court Tuesday for misclassifying the annotators as independent contractors. The proposed class action alleges Surge AI deliberately misclassified its workers, denying them key benefits afforded to employees while the company receives a “major windfall in labor costs.”. The annotators, who are tasked with AI training projects for client companies including Meta Platforms Inc. and OpenAI Inc., lack protections afforded under California labor law, including minimum wage, overtime, and meal and rest break periods, the lawsuit said. ## Learn more about Bloomberg Law or Log In to keep reading:. Log in to keep reading or access research tools and resources. Go to Bloomberg Law News.
